symbols: standing female figure — the Virgin Mary reimagined through a contemporary lens of embodied humanity
exposed musculature and raw flesh — the vulnerability and suffering of the human condition
description: A contemporary sculpture by Kiki Smith depicting the Virgin Mary as a full-length standing female figure on a dark base. The figure is rendered with exposed musculature and raw, reddish-brown surface texture suggesting flayed skin or anatomical transparency. The body stands upright and frontal, arms at the sides with a dignified posture. The head is smooth and simplified. The figure combines classical contrapposto with a disturbing anatomical realism that strips away the idealized surface to reveal the flesh beneath.
theological_description: Kiki Smith's provocative reinterpretation of the Virgin Mary strips away centuries of idealized representation to present Mary in her radical humanity — the body that bore the incarnate God rendered not as porcelain perfection but as exposed, vulnerable flesh. This resonates with the theology of Galatians 4:4: 'God sent His Son, born of woman' — Mary's body as the actual, physical site of the Incarnation. The visible musculature and raw surface suggest the cost of bearing and losing a divine Son, fulfilling Simeon's prophecy in Luke 2:35 that a sword would pierce her own soul. The sculpture embodies the Christian affirmation of the body's dignity — not despite its vulnerability but through it. As 2 Corinthians 4:7 declares, 'We have this treasure in jars of clay,' and Mary's clay vessel contained the greatest treasure of all. The unflinching exposure of the body's reality also connects to Job 10:11, 'You clothed me with skin and flesh and knit me together with bones and sinews,' a prayer of wonder at the embodied creation through which God chose to act.

scripture_refs.reason: Born of woman — Mary's physical body as the site of the Incarnation, rendered in raw materiality
A sword will pierce your own soul — the exposed flesh suggesting suffering and vulnerability
Treasure in jars of clay — the divine mystery housed in fragile human flesh
